There's pros and cons of both of them.

The shovel phase is nice because here's this abstraction layer that I can just write things too.
It has some fault-tarring guarantees that they're not going to be able to do.
There's that. But now there's a whole other service I got to run with additional nodes
and essentially now making another copy of data.
So there's pros and cons. There's no free lunch.

or maybe move workloads between the cloud and on-prem.

The question is, is there still a big market for people running on-prem databases?
Yes.
But the sales cycle is for those things, which is way different.
Because you've got to go out and have a go-fly out there and talk to the customer,
take them out to dinner and go golfing, you know, like f***ing things in the 80s.

I mean, there's everybody...

I certainly can run it cheaper than what Amazon would charge me for machines or, right?
But then, like, then you've got to pay for humans to go actually and manage those things.

Okay, so the abstraction, they're going to have data storage, they're going to have this notion of a virtual warehouse.
Again, this is how they first designed it where you basically say, I want to...
You don't say exactly on our node, say I want to sort of this compute capacity.
If you say, here's some virtual data warehouse that I can give me an endpoint where I can start sending data into and run queries on.
So when you turn on a virtual data warehouse, whether or not you're running queries, you're always paying for it.
And we'll see how to know if they could leverage that when they do the flexible compute,
because they can steal all your cycles from the warehouses.
In 2022, they added support for serverless deployments.
So now basically, the virtual data warehouse spins itself down if you're not running any queries.
But obviously, they charge a premium for that.
Because now you're using more shared infrastructure at the cloud service layer instead of spinning that up yourself.
And then the cloud service layer is just the catch all phrase for the front end of the system that encompasses all the things that we've been talking about in the entire semester.
Some coordinator, scheduler, the catalog, the query optimizer, all that is the entry point for queries.
And let's see at the end of the semester, sorry, in the class, the catalog is interesting because they're going to be built on another data system called FoundationDB,
that provides them transaction semantics for doing updates.
So now within a, at the compute layer, they have a notion of team, a worker node, and a worker process.
And again, this is from 2012, 2013.
So a worker node, at least in the original version, is just an EC2 instance.
Right? This is for Docker, this is for Kubernetes.
Docker is 2012, Kubernetes is 2014, but in 2012, you had all EC2 instances.
And so on that instance, this is where they're going to maintain this local cache on the attached storage device of that instance.
So not reading writing to EBS, it's always running on a local, or now the local SSD.
And this cache will be a combination of intermediate results that you're generating for a query while it's running, as well as some of the persistent files that you maybe are treating from S3.
So the idea is that if another query shows up, it reads the same data that you just read from S3.
I can read it from my local cache, which would be faster and cheaper than having to go to a round trip lookup on over S3 or whatever the after-store is.

Again, we'll see this in a second. The way they're going to manage this, keep this everything consistent, or keep spread data out evenly, and be able to scale up and scale down without having to reshuffle everything as you would in a shared nothing architecture, is that they're going to rely on consistent hash and to keep track of what worker node is responsible for what persistent data on in their own cache.
And then within the worker node, when a query shows up, the fire off a whole new worker process, literally like a spawn of a new process in the OS.

And that's going to be executing whatever the task are for this query. And it can read write data to inmate results and other workers or out the S3. And then when the query is done, the process ends and goes away.
Yes.
It's like a tangential question, but you can have an EBS volume under the root as the root device, right? So is that not like a local, you mean, locally connected?

This question is, on ECT, you can have an EBS volume mounted as the root device. Yes, you have to, anyway, because if that, like an AMI image has to spin up, but you still kind of locally attached SSD that you can then use NVMe or whatever to 2012 before that, like that you can then read right locally. That's just another mountain of file system.
And that's way faster than EBS.

Actually, so I don't know whether they've switched over to Kubernetes now. We'll see it in, we'll tell you what, yellow brick, the paper, they're all in on Kubernetes and they make a big deal about how they're designed to run in the sort of the Kubernetes infrastructure.
I assume you now they're not dumb that they're doing something very similar these days, whether it's exactly Kubernetes or something else.

So when a query actually starts running, they're going to be doing a push-based vectorized execution using the pre-compile primitives with template and C++ based on the different data types that we've talked about.
We've already mentioned that they're only doing code gen for when they serialize and de-serialize data going from one worker node to another.

And as he mentioned, they're not going to do explicit shuffle between stages and instead the worker processes are going to be allowed to send data directly to push the data directly to the next node who's going to process it or to keep it locally and keep processing it if they're going up the pipeline as further as needed.
So that means now when if the worker node has all the intimate results, if it crashes or there's a failure, it's not replicated or it's not stored as an external service from the worker node.

So that means the computation is lost. And unlike in Dremel and Spark where if one worker goes down, then the coordinator then invokes a new owner hands off that task to another worker, what Snowflake would do is kill the entire query and then restart it from the beginning.

And that's actually how the people built OLAP systems back in the day. I mentioned this before in with MapReduce, they were storing things on disk as they went along but they had the ability to kill tasks, it reacts to things and do basically partial retry.

And in the Snowflake world, they're not going to add any of that infrastructure because that's additional engineering complexity, they're just going to make the decision, okay, well one worker failed, okay, just kill the whole thing and restart.
And who pays for the restart? The customer.

Right? So obviously, you know, they're not killing nodes randomly, right? They have a blog article when they discussed like, okay, like if a worker dies, they have to identify, is this something that we did or is this a transit network failure?
Right? In some cases, they can actually automatically roll you back to a previous version of Snowflake and rerun that query, rerun the query, see whether that solves the problem.
The tricky thing also too is like, if now you're ingesting new data, you want to make sure that the query when it reruns, like is it seeing the same data that I had before? Question or?
How often do query like these queries fail?
Question is how often do queries fail? I mean, not that often.
Right? I... Actually, they have a blog article I should link. I don't know if it's that number, but it's not like, you know, one out of ten. It's some way smaller fraction.

Again, because it's like, you're not running, again, just going back to the map-produced world, like Hadoop was talking about, okay, we're going to run this query for this map-produced job, 1,000 of like cheap pizza box machines, like the one unit rack machines.
And in that environment, yeah, one of them is going to go down, if I have queries going to...
